chunk_summary: Assessment of breastfeeding adequacy in infants under six months involves monitoring growth, feeding frequency, diaper output, and satiety cues, alongside addressing feeding challenges through observation and intervention.
learning_objectives: Understand key indicators of breastfeeding adequacy in infants under six months.
Identify red flags and reversible causes of poor milk transfer during breastfeeding.
Learn practical interventions to support breastfeeding and ensure adequate milk intake.
Recognize when to escalate concerns to lactation support or medical evaluation.
key_facts: Infants should regain birth weight by 10–14 days; failure warrants urgent review.
Adequate milk transfer is indicated by at least 6 wet diapers per day after day 5.
Feeding frequency in early infancy is typically 8–12 feeds per 24 hours, including night feeds.
Satiety cues include relaxed hands, spontaneous unlatching, and calm sleep.
Red flags include persistent fussing after feeds, very sleepy feeds, or prolonged feeding (>40 minutes) with minimal swallowing.
Conditions like nasal obstruction, oral thrush, or ankyloglossia can impair milk transfer.
reference_quote: ‘Failure to regain birth weight by 10–14 days or crossing percentiles downward warrants urgent review.’ (Source: WHO - Infant and young child feeding)
